summary: On September 11, 2001, I witnessed the horrific events unfold as planes hit the World Trade Center buildings. I saw people jumping from the towers and the second plane hitting the building, which was a terrifying and surreal experience. Later, I helped evacuees from Manhattan on Ellis Island, taking down their information and assisting wherever I could. The day was filled with shock, fear, and a strong sense of unity among everyone involved.

The United States Park Police played a crucial role on 9/11 by stopping a suspicious speedboat that was heading towards us at full throttle near the flagpole. Their quick action helped ease our fears of another attack. They were also involved in managing the boats and people arriving on Ellis Island during the evacuation efforts. Their presence was a reassuring part of the chaotic day.

I live on Staten Island, and after the events of 9/11, I had to get home with a police escort due to roadblocks and security measures. From Ellis Island, I could look out and see Staten Island across the water, which looked peaceful and beautiful that day despite the tragedy. Staten Island was my safe haven after a very difficult day.

Manhattan was the epicenter of the tragedy on 9/11, especially around the World Trade Center area. Many people were evacuated from Manhattan to Ellis Island, including those injured or just displaced. I saw the black smoke rising from Manhattan and the destruction of the buildings, which was surreal and heartbreaking. The evacuation efforts involved many agencies working together to help those fleeing Manhattan.

The World Trade Center was where the planes hit and the buildings collapsed on 9/11. I saw the impact and the fireball from the second plane hitting the building, and later witnessed the towers falling. Many people escaped from the nearby hotels and offices, and evacuees from the World Trade Center area were brought to Ellis Island for processing and care. The devastation there was overwhelming and unforgettable.

Katherine Taylor was a woman from the American Family Immigration History Center who stayed with us on Ellis Island during the evacuation. She shared with me a memorable story about a family of six who escaped from a hotel near the World Trade Center just before the collapse. Katherine's presence and stories helped me understand the human side of the tragedy and the many personal experiences of those affected.

On 9/11, the Staten Island Ferry stopped running for a while as the evacuation and emergency response unfolded. I noticed the usual orange ferry boats were not operating back and forth across the harbor. Later, the ferry resumed service but only to bring emergency personnel into Manhattan, not civilians. The ferry's halt was a visible sign of how the city was affected that day.

Liberty State Park in Jersey City, New Jersey, was one of the places where evacuees from Manhattan were transported after arriving on Ellis Island. Many people who came off the boats were bused there for further care and processing. It was part of the larger effort to manage the displaced and injured after the attacks, helping to provide some order amid the chaos.

Some evacuees from Manhattan were offered boat transportation to Brooklyn after arriving on Ellis Island. Brooklyn was one of the destinations used to help manage the large number of people displaced by the attacks. It was part of the coordinated response to ensure people were moved safely and efficiently away from the disaster area.

Aramark, the food concessionaire here, played an important role on 9/11 by providing tons of water and food to staff and evacuees on Ellis Island. The manager of the gift shop opened up the food service and helped prepare sandwiches along with our staff. Their support helped sustain everyone who was working and those who had just arrived, showing how everyone pulled together that day.

The New York City Police Department was heavily involved on 9/11, with officers from various boroughs, including the Bronx, brought in to assist. I spoke with some Bronx officers who were preparing to help with identifying remains at the morgue area near the docks. The department's detectives, sergeants, men, and women showed great dedication and were part of the massive response effort that day.

Police officers from the Bronx were among those who came to assist on 9/11. I talked to several of them who were preparing to work at the morgue area to help identify remains from the disaster. Their presence was part of the broader New York City Police Department response, showing how the entire city mobilized to handle the crisis.

Jersey City, New Jersey, was significant on 9/11 because evacuees from Manhattan were transported to Liberty State Park there after arriving on Ellis Island. It served as a key location for managing and caring for displaced people during the aftermath of the attacks. Jersey City was part of the regional response that helped support New York City in this tragic time.
